advantages: Covariance captures joint variation in original units, useful when magnitude of co-movement matters for modeling or reporting.
Pearson correlation is scale-free and bounded, enabling fair comparison of linear relationships across features measured in different units.
The covariance matrix compactly encodes all pairwise second-moment structure needed for PCA, Gaussian models, and quadratic distances.
Correlation matrices are easy to inspect (diagonal 1, off-diagonals in [-1, 1]) for redundancy and multicollinearity screening.
disadvantages: Both measures target linear association; non-linear dependencies can yield correlation near zero despite strong structure.
Sensitive to outliers—one extreme point can inflate covariance and correlation substantially.
Sample covariance matrices can be singular or ill-conditioned when n < p or features are nearly redundant.
Correlation does not imply causation; high |r| may reflect confounding, common causes, or selection bias rather than a direct effect.
